data.title: Tesla jumps 22% as it headlines a big day of tariff relief in the beat-up EV sector
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) led the electric vehicle sector higher on Wednesday after President Donald Trump dropped new tariff rates on imports from most U.S. trade partners to 10% for 90 days to set up more time for trade negotiations.

While Trump said imports from China will be hit with tariffs of 125% effective immediately, investors seem to be assuming that the U.S. and China will start to negotiate seriously. The risk of a trade war between the U.S. and China is a major consideration in the EV industry due to the reliance on China-made components and supply chain implications. 

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) was up 21.7% at 3:50 p.m. to put shares back to where they stood on April 3. Trading volume on Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) was over 173 million shares as the afternoon tariff announcement sent jolts across trading desks.

Other notable gainers in the EV sector included Polestar Automotive (PSNY) +18%, Lucid Group (LCID) +10.2%, Rivian Automotive (RIVN) +8.8%, VinFast Auto (VFS) +8.1%, Lotus Technology (LOT) +7.6%, Li Auto (LI) +7.1%, XPeng (XPEV) +6.5%, NIO (NIO) +5.8%, and ZEEKR Intelligent (ZK) +4.4%. Similar to Tesla (TSLA), most of those names were recouping losses built up over the last week and are still in negative territory for the year.

EV-related suppliers Stoneridge (SRI) +19.0%, REE Automotive (REE) +16.9%, QuantumScape (QS) +16.6%, and Hyliion Holdings (HYLN) +11.7% were also significant advancers off the tariff developments.

data.title: A US-built iPhone could cost 90% more, BofA says
data.description:
Following yesterday's comments from the White House that Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) could build an iPhone in the U.S., Bank of America said an endeavor is “technically possible,” but it would come at an enormous cost.

Shares soared 15% on Wednesday, after President Trump said he would pause most tariffs for 90 days.

“In our opinion, iPhone cost can increase 25% purely on higher labor cost in the U.S.,” analyst Wamsi Mohan wrote in a note to clients. “On top of that, if Apple had to pay reciprocal tariffs to import sub-assemblies into the U.S., we see the total cost of an iPhone increasing 90%+.”

Mohan, who has a Buy rating and $250 price target on Apple, took his estimates based on the iPhone 16 Pro Max.

Assuming Apple could find the labor in the U.S. to assemble iPhones, Mohan said that a “significant” portion of the sub-assemblies would still need to be manufactured elsewhere, assembled in China and then imported to the U.S. “This is because, while it may be possible to move final assembly to the U.S., moving the entire iPhone supply chain would be a much bigger undertaking and would likely take many years, if even possible,” Mohan explained.

Despite efforts to diversify its supply chain geographically, Apple still relies on China for a significant portion of its manufacturing needs, specifically iPhones. Investment firm Needham recently estimated that Apple's fiscal 2025 earnings could be negatively impacted by 28% or more in a trade war between China and the U.S. unless it was given an exemption.

Aside from China, Apple and its partners manufacture iPhones, AirPods, Apple Watches, Macs and iPads in several countries, including India, Vietnam and Brazil. All of these countries have also been hit with tariffs by President Trump.

Apple releases its second quarter fiscal 2025 financial results on May 1. A consensus estimate expects Apple to report earnings per share of $1.61 on revenue of $94.04B.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, and Dow rise despite China placing 84% tariffs on U.S. products
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages rise on Wednesday despite China reportedly slapped 84% tariffs on U.S. products after the Trump administration went ahead with its 104% levy on Chinese goods.

Early on and the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.4%, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 9 basis points to 4.38% and at one point pushed up to 4.52%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.77%.

In response to the U.S.'s 104% tariffs on Chinese goods, Beijing will raise its tariff on U.S. goods to 84%, effective April 10, the government said on Wednesday, according to media reports.

Another topsy-turvy trading session on Wall Street saw the major averages rally furiously at the open, then steadily give up gains and turn negative, followed by a period of fluctuation. They then decidedly turned lower and eventually ended deep in the red.

"Several triggers drove a sharp turn lower in market sentiment as the day went on. First, we had confirmation from U.S. officials that the additional 50% tariff that Trump threatened China with on Monday would be imposed, dashing any remaining hopes for a last-minute extension before the deadline," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"The market turmoil has shown no sign of letting up over the last 24 hours, with the S&P 500 edging increasingly close to bear market territory, having now shed -18.9% since its peak in mid-February. Moreover, futures are still pointing towards further losses today,"  Allen said.

Trump on Tuesday also said that the U.S. will soon introduce a "major" tariff on pharmaceutical imports.

"Trump implemented an extraordinary tax increase on US consumers overnight. If the 104% tax increase on goods from China is not reversed, it is likely to push the US into recession more rapidly," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

For years, the U.S. persuaded foreigners to exchange U.S. government promises for valuable goods and services. That exchange is threatened—despite equities falling yesterday, bond yields rose sharply, Donovan added.

The results of $39B, 10-year note auction and the FOMC minutes will land later in the day on Wednesday.

data.title: Tariff wars: China slams Trump's 104% levies, vows retaliation
Endeavour Silver surges after Trump's tariff pause, Q1 production results
data.description:
Endeavour Silver (NYSE:EXK) +16.2% in Wednesday's trading after President Trump issued a 90-day pause on new tariff rates on imports from most U.S. trade partners, and the company reported Q1 production of slightly more than 1.2M silver oz and 8,338 gold oz, for silver equivalent output of 1.9M oz; the stock had gained modestly before Trump's announcement.

Endeavour (NYSE:EXK) said consolidated silver production was in line with its plan and 17% lower than the year-earlier quarter, driven by 24% lower silver output at the Guanaceví mine, partially offset by 53% higher silver production at the Bolañitos mine compared to the same quarter last year.

Q1 consolidated gold production was 18% lower than the prior-year quarter due to 28% lower gold output at the Bolañitos mine and 3% lower gold production at Guanaceví.

Guanaceví throughput was 11% lower than a year ago, and silver and gold grades were 13% lower and 4% higher, respectively, which drove the lower silver and gold output; the mine's throughput and grades, however, were in line with plan.

Bolañitos throughput was in line with a year earlier, combined with 60% higher silver grades offset by 26% lower gold grades; throughput and silver grades were consistent with plan, while gold grades were slightly lower than plan.

data.title: University Bancorp raises stake in Currency Exchange
data.description:
• Bank holding company University Bancorp (OTCQB:UNIB) announced on Wednesday the acquisition of 6,200 shares of Currency Exchange International for C$124,310.
  • The acquisition was made through the facilities of the Toronto Stock Exchange at an average price of C$20.05 per share.
  • The share purchase represents an increase of ~0.10% in UNIB's holdings, with the total holdings now coming at ~10% of the issued and outstanding shares of Currency Exchange.

data.title: U.S. bank stocks jump on back of Trump's tariff pause
data.description:
U.S. bank stocks surged with the broader equity market after President Donald Trump announced a 90-day pause on tit-for-tat tariffs for more than 75 countries but a higher levy on Chinese imports.

The overall financials sector (NYSEARCA:XLF) advanced 7.3% (vs.S&P 500's 9% surge) at press time, but still remained below where it was on Trump's April 2 Liberation Day announcement.

Megabanks scored significant intraday gains, with Cirigroup (NYSE:C) surging 9.6%, Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) +5.8%, JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) +7.9%, Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) +6.6%, Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) +11% and Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) +11.6%.

Regional banks: U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) +7.8%, PNC Financial (NYSE:PNC) +6.6%, KeyCorp (NYSE:KEY) +9.8%, Webster Financial (NYSE:WBS) +11%, First Horizon (NYSE:FHN) +9.5%, Fifth Third Bancorp (NASDAQ:FITB) +7.5% and Huntington Bancshares (NASDAQ:HBAN) +9.8%.

Custodial banks: Bank of New York Mellon (BK) +7.8%, State Street (STT) +8.8% and Northern Trust (NTRS) +10%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Goldman Sachs rescinds recession baseline hours after announcing it amid tariff turmoil
data.description:
Goldman Sachs rescinded their recession base case within a few hours.

Right below 1 p.m., its economists wrote that they expected to change their forecast for the U.S. economy to a recession if the country-specific tariffs scheduled for Wednesday took effect.

Goldman economists then moved to forecast a 65% probability of a recession in the next 12 months.

But as President Donald Trump announced on X that he will initiate a 90-day pause on the additional country-specific “reciprocal” tariffs, Goldman economists reverted their non-recession baseline.

“Together, these tariffs are likely to sum to something close to our previous expectation of a 15 percentage points increase in the effective tariff rate,” said Jan Hatzius, chief economist and head of Global Investment Research at Goldman Sachs. “As a result, we are reverting to our previous non-recession baseline forecast, with GDP growth of 0.5% and a 45% probability of recession.”

“The market roller coaster does not surprise me,” said Mohamed El-Erian, president of Queens’ College at Cambridge University. “Indeed, it is to be widely expected. This does surprise me. A recession call – on or off – should not be this volatile or this dependent on a single policy issue, even if it's tariffs.”

President Trump also announced in his message that the tariff rate on imports from China will rise further to 125%.

data.title: Memory stocks in focus as Google says AI hypercomputer runs on new TPU
data.description:
• Memory stocks were in focus on Wednesday as Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (GOOGL) said its new artificial intelligence hypercomputer will run on its latest memory-heavy tensor processing unit.
  • Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) shares rose 16.8% in late trading, while American Depository Receipts of Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and SK Hnix (OTCPK:HXSCF) were little changed.
  • “Our new seventh-generation TPU, Ironwood, is designed specifically for thinking and inferential AI models,” Google said in a blog post, referencing its latest TPU. “Ironwood offers five times more peak compute capacity and six times the high-bandwidth memory (HBM) capacity compared to the prior-generation TPU.”
  • Micron, Samsung and SK Hnix largely control the memory market, according to research firm TechInsights.

data.title: McDonald's is beating the broad market this year amid wild tariff swings
data.description:
McDonald's Corporation (NYSE:MCD) trickled lower in afternoon trading as the restaurant stock continued to avoid the wild swings seen over the last week in the broad stock market. McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) is still up 3.3% on a year-to-date basis, despite recession fears nipping at shares over the last week. The YTD return is good enough to top broad market averages by a wide margin.

 

 

 

As for tariffs, McDonald's (MCD) does not need to make major changes, although it sources a portion of its beef from Australia, which is now subject to a 10% tariff under recent U.S. trade policies. However, an extended trade war could impact the company's international growth plans and could increase the risk of a recession in the U.S. Some analysts have also pointed out that anti-U.S. backlash arising from tariff policies could also hurt McDonald's (MCD) international sales and expansion efforts.

McDonald's (MCD) is due to report earnings in early May. The restaurant company has missed EPS estimates in three of the last four earnings reports. 

On Wall Street, 24 out of 38 firms covering McDonald's (MCD) have a Buy-equivalent rating. Seeking Alpha analysts are more cautious, with an equal number of Buy, Hold, and Sell ratings on the books over the last 90 days.
